item.title: Why ‘rightwing’ is no longer such a dirty word in Britain
item.description: When words like fascist and Nazi are thrown around, being merely conservative seems mild by comparison

item.title: AI should have senior lawyers sharpening their hunting spears
item.description: More adoption of artificial intelligence will lead to more calls to cut fees

item.title: The art of knowing when to step down
item.description: Only a few master one of the hardest career tricks of them all

item.title: Chips are nearly five times as volatile as the broader market
item.description: Semiconductor stock roulette

item.title: The London IPO market isn’t as bad as it looks
item.description: It is easy to look enviously at New York, but the UK is on course to enjoy its best year for new listings since 2021

item.title: Your odyssey to see ‘The Odyssey’ matters
item.description: The extremes that film fanatics are going to in order to watch Nolan’s latest tell us something

item.title: How Tour de France can fix its penny-farthing-shaped pay problem
item.description: A fairer distribution of prize money among the cyclists would make this event’s wheels go round more smoothly

item.title: How long until there is a US markets reckoning over Trump’s damage?
item.description: The patience of the bond markets will ultimately wear thin

item.title: Exit, pursued by a capybara
item.description: What do silly-season stories tell us about the state we’re in?

item.title: Ageism: the silent but deadly threat to your retirement
item.description: For Gen X, financial planning needs to involve career planning

item.title: Eben Sadie: ‘Culture, not science, makes wine’
item.description: South Africa’s pioneering winemaker, a driving force behind the country’s new wave of winemaking, talks research, instinct and succession

item.title: The art of joy, and how to learn it
item.description: It makes our lives more vibrant — and we can find it in the most day-to-day experiences

item.title: A licence to dislike
item.description: The lifestyle gurus say we should be open to new experiences, regardless of vintage. To which I say, sod that

item.title: Jul’s, London: strange food at serious prices — restaurant review
item.description: A restaurant quoting Theodore Zeldin on gastronomic joy serves a succession of costly, confused dishes that are more likely to induce gloom

item.title: Does your financial relationship with your partner need a reset?
item.description: A good financial planning session can sometimes feel like couples counselling

item.title: How long can oil markets absorb the Hormuz shock?
item.description: Improvements in oil intensity have cushioned the global economy so far — but could prompt reckless political choices

item.title: Britain’s Burnham and nostalgia for a semi-imaginary past
item.description: The new prime minister has to set out a coherent and business-friendly platform

item.title: SpaceX and the myth of independent Wall St research
item.description: Buy recommendations from IPO banks underscore the limits of post-dotcom reforms

item.title: Andy Burnham will find that hope is not enough to govern
item.description: Once in Downing Street, the new prime minister should tell the Labour Party what it doesn’t want to hear

item.title: The perils of relying on PE ratios
item.description: Global gyrations in stock prices and earnings favour other markets

item.title: What are the rules for power napping?
item.description: How to make the benefits of the recuperative 20 winks appreciated in your office

item.title: The ‘go big or stay home’ economy reshaping live music
item.description: Consumers will spend multiples of an annual streaming subscription for three hours of music at a giant stadium

item.title: Who pays for AI?
item.description: And how much?

item.title: A Europe that’s losing at tech could still achieve quantum supremacy
item.description: The UK and EU have committed twice as much money as the US to investments in the computing sector

item.title: Why Iran is returning to war
item.description: Its rulers believe that further escalation will force the US to offer the security and economic relief it craves
